toolSelectionRules.reason: Tier 1 tools are pure Node.js, work everywhere, and are safest. Always prefer these first.
Tier 2 tools have common npm dependencies. Use if Tier 1 tools don't meet your needs.
Tier 3 tools handle code execution, browser automation, and environment checks. Uses QuickJS/Pyodide WASM sandbox for safe JS/Python execution. Bash is not supported in sandboxed mode.
Tier 4 tools have heavy dependencies (100MB+ models, ffmpeg, whisper.cpp, OAuth2). Only use when explicitly needed.

behavioralGuidance: Always prefer simpler toolkit tools (Tier 1) over run_command for file operations.
Never write complex inline PowerShell arrays through run_command — they break escaping and corrupt files.
Use run_command only when external binaries must run natively or shell-specific features are required.
For text-heavy operations (markdown, JSON, code), always use write_file or edit_file — never try to pipe content through run_command.
Check tool prerequisites before using Tier 3+ tools to avoid silent failures.
If a tool returns success: false, always check the 'hint' field for installation or troubleshooting instructions.
For semantic search, build the index (index) first, then query it (index_query) — don't query before indexing.
For browser automation, prefer fetch_web_content for simple pages, browser only for JavaScript-rendered content.
For code execution, prefer execute_code with sandboxing over raw shell commands — QuickJS/Pyodide WASM provides isolation.
Always use dryRun mode (write_file, edit_file, read_file) to preview changes before applying them.

When using execute_code with PowerShell, avoid these patterns that break escaping: (1) Here-strings (@'...') require multi-line input, (2) Regex character classes like ['\"] get parsed as separate arguments, (3) Single quotes inside single-quoted strings need doubling (''). Use the powershell_utils.ts module for safe patterns.
For Windows file operations, prefer 'cmd' shell over 'powershell' shell when running npm/npx commands — the npm.ps1 wrapper has known $LASTEXITCODE variable issues on some Windows configurations.
For build commands, execute .cmd files directly (e.g., .\node_modules\.bin\tsc.cmd) rather than through npm/npx to avoid PowerShell shim issues.
Use the grep tool instead of Select-String for pattern searching — Select-String regex patterns get corrupted when passed through JSON to shell.
For cross-platform compatibility, prefer PowerShell-native commands (Get-ChildItem, Get-Content, etc.) over bash commands on Windows.
When searching for imports or patterns, use grep tool with appropriate flags instead of trying to build complex regex patterns through execute_code.
For file operations that require path manipulation, use write_file/create_file tools rather than trying to construct file paths through shell commands.

knownIssues.issue: Require the closing @' on its own line with no trailing whitespace. execute_code passes code as a single command string, breaking multi-line syntax.
knownIssues.workaround: Use write_file tool for file content, or use Set-Content with -Value parameter

knownIssues.issue: The ] character is interpreted as a command-line token by the tool's argument parser, causing 'positional parameter cannot be found' errors.
knownIssues.workaround: Use -SimpleMatch flag with Select-String, or use grep tool instead

knownIssues.issue: PowerShell requires doubling single quotes ('') to represent a literal single quote inside single-quoted strings.
PowerShell interprets $ as variable expansion in double-quoted strings, corrupting the intended output.
knownIssues.workaround: Escape $ as $$ in double-quoted strings, or use single-quoted strings

knownIssues.issue: PowerShell uses backticks as escape characters in double-quoted strings.
knownIssues.workaround: Escape backtick as `` in double-quoted strings

safePatterns: Use write_file/edit_file for all file content operations
Use grep tool for pattern searching instead of Select-String
Use cmd shell for npm/npx commands to avoid $LASTEXITCODE issues
Use execute_code with shell='cmd' for Windows path operations
Use powershell_utils.ts helper functions for complex escaping scenarios

js.security: Default-deny, explicit opt-in for network/fs, audit logging enabled

python.security: Isolated CPython runtime, no native OS access

bash.reason: Shell scripting requires a real underlying OS
